title: Negative Edge
description: Third-person action arena shooter developed in Unreal Engine 5. I worked as Technical Lead and Lead Gameplay/Systems Programmer, building the core gameplay architecture and coordinating the technical integration of design, animation, art, audio and production.
aboutHtml:
Negative Edge is a third-person action arena shooter focused on fast movement, ranged combat and a physics-driven grappling hook. Its gameplay alternates between exploration, combat encounters and story-driven progression across a sequence of interconnected environments.

I worked on the project as Technical Lead and Lead Gameplay/Systems Programmer. I designed and implemented a large part of the game’s core technical foundation, including player movement, camera behavior, aiming assistance, interaction, character states, combat resources, enemy encounters, progression events and several tools used by the design team.

My responsibilities extended beyond programming. I acted as the main technical point of reference across the team, evaluating the feasibility of proposed features, supporting designers and artists during implementation, coordinating the integration of assets and systems, resolving source-control conflicts and producing updated builds.

My role was ultimately to connect the team’s creative vision with its practical execution, balancing gameplay quality, technical sustainability, production time and the resources available to a small independent team.

sections.title: MY ROLE AND OWNERSHIP
sections.html:
My contribution to Negative Edge was not limited to implementing isolated programming tasks. I took ownership of features throughout their entire lifecycle: from the initial idea and feasibility discussion to implementation, integration, testing and refinement inside the final gameplay experience.

During team meetings, I was one of the main voices responsible for evaluating whether proposed mechanics, content and production choices were technically achievable. This involved estimating complexity, identifying dependencies, considering the available development time and determining whether an idea could be implemented with the team’s current resources.

When necessary, I suggested alternative solutions or reduced the scope of a feature while preserving its original design intention. In other cases, I gave the team the technical confidence to proceed with ideas that were ambitious but realistically achievable.

I also tried to promote a product-oriented approach to development. Completing an assigned task was not considered enough if the result had not been tested, integrated or evaluated within the broader context of the game. I encouraged team members to think beyond the immediate task, identify missing elements, verify their work in-engine and take greater ownership of the final result.

This approach influenced both my own work and my collaboration with the rest of the team. My objective was not simply to produce individual features, but to ensure that every contribution moved the project towards a more coherent, stable and complete product.

sections.title: CORE GAMEPLAY AND SYSTEMS ARCHITECTURE
sections.html:
I designed and implemented a large part of the reusable gameplay architecture supporting Negative Edge. These systems were built to keep individual features modular while allowing them to communicate and cooperate within a shared framework.

Player Control and Game Feel

The systems most directly responsible for controlling the player and shaping the moment-to-moment experience include:

  • Custom Movement System — An extension of Unreal Engine’s Character Movement Component, used to support the game’s custom locomotion requirements and movement-related mechanics.
  • Dynamic Camera System — An adaptive third-person camera framework that changes its behavior according to player actions and gameplay states, using smooth transitions between camera configurations.
  • Aim Assist System — A context-aware targeting assistance system designed to improve aiming accuracy and usability when playing with a gamepad.
  • Grappling Hook System — A physics-driven traversal mechanic that manages target detection, pulling behavior, movement transitions and the interaction between grappling and the player’s existing movement state.
  • Input Mapping System — A flexible input mapping system for easy reconfiguration.

These systems were developed together rather than as completely isolated features. Movement, aiming, camera behavior and grappling all influence one another, so particular attention was given to transitions and to preventing one system from producing unexpected behavior inside another.

Shared Gameplay Foundations

I also developed several general-purpose frameworks used across different parts of the project:

  • State Machine System — A structured state-management framework used to organize actor behavior and define consistent transitions between gameplay states.
  • Resource Attribute System — A modular system for handling gameplay resources and statistics such as health, stamina and shields.
  • Global Events System — A centralized communication layer for broadcasting and receiving game-wide events while reducing unnecessary direct dependencies between systems.
  • Interaction System — A context-based framework that allows the player to detect and interact with objects and entities in the game world.
  • Pop-Up System — A modular UI system used to display notifications, prompts and other contextual information.

These systems provided common rules and interfaces that could be reused across gameplay features. Their purpose was not only to solve individual problems, but also to reduce duplicated logic and make the project easier to extend.

Contextual and Environmental Systems

Additional systems were developed to allow the game to understand and react to the player’s surroundings:

  • Environmental Scanner System — Detects nearby objects, surfaces and interactable elements, exposing contextual information to other gameplay systems.
  • Environmental Audio System — Determines the type of space currently occupied by the player, such as an open area, interior, underground space or cave, and communicates this information to the audio implementation.
  • World Trigger System — A reusable framework for activating events, progression logic and scripted sequences inside the game world.

Together, these systems allowed gameplay, audio and progression logic to respond dynamically to the player’s position and actions.

sections.title: ENCOUNTERS AND ENEMY SPAWNING
sections.html:
I developed the Encounter System used to control enemy combat sequences throughout the game. The system combines wave management, enemy spawning and encounter progression into a reusable framework that can support different combat scenarios.

The Wave Manager controls the structure and completion conditions of an encounter. Depending on the configuration, a wave can wait for all enemies to be defeated, advance after a timer or follow other event-driven conditions.

The Spawn Manager is responsible for determining how and where enemies are introduced into the level. It supports different spawning approaches and provides designers with configurable tools for building encounters without requiring new code for every combat area.

The complete system was designed to manage:

  • enemy registration and tracking;
  • wave activation and completion;
  • multiple spawning configurations;
  • transitions between consecutive waves;
  • encounter start and end events;
  • communication with doors, UI, audio and progression systems;
  • protection against invalid or incomplete encounter states.

A major part of this work involved handling cases that are easy to overlook during the initial design of an encounter. Examples include enemies being removed unexpectedly, overlapping wave events, players entering or leaving an area at unusual times and progression logic being triggered in a different order from the expected one.

The goal was to provide designers with enough flexibility to create different combat situations while maintaining reliable rules for how each encounter begins, progresses and ends.

sections.title: GAMEPLAY FLOW AND TECHNICAL DESIGN
sections.html:
Beyond the development of individual systems, I was heavily involved in constructing the overall game loop and implementing the progression of the player through the world.

I assembled the initial tutorial and implemented the gameplay events required to guide the player through its different stages. I also worked on the sequences connecting exploration, combat encounters, environmental events and access to new areas.

To support this process, I created the World Trigger System and exposed it to the design team through accessible Unreal Engine tools. Designers could use it to create the first version of a gameplay sequence and communicate the intended player experience without depending on a programmer for every individual event.

Once the initial flow had been created, I reviewed the implementation from a technical and systemic perspective. This involved considering not only the intended path, but also the many alternative ways in which a real player might approach the same situation.

Typical cases included:

  • entering an area from an unexpected direction;
  • activating events in a different order;
  • leaving an encounter before its completion;
  • triggering the same event multiple times;
  • skipping an expected interaction;
  • reaching a location before the intended progression state;
  • defeating enemies before all related events had been initialized;
  • reloading the game during an intermediate state.

I used these cases to identify possible soft locks, hard locks, repeated events and inconsistent world states. I then refined the initial design implementation so that it remained functional even when the player behaved differently from the ideal sequence imagined during planning.

This work placed me between gameplay programming and technical design. My responsibility was not only to reproduce the intended experience, but to convert it into a robust set of rules capable of supporting actual player behavior.

sections.title: CROSS-DISCIPLINARY TECHNICAL DIRECTION
sections.html:
I acted as a technical point of reference for the different disciplines working on the project. A significant part of my role involved translating creative requirements into practical specifications and helping each department understand how its work would be used inside Unreal Engine.

Game Design

I supported designers in the use of Blueprint, Unreal Engine tools and the custom systems developed for the project. I helped convert high-level gameplay ideas into implementable logic and reviewed proposed flows to identify technical risks, missing conditions and possible soft locks or hard locks.

UI and 2D Art

I provided technical guidelines regarding file formats, resolutions, dimensions and asset preparation for UI elements, textures and other 2D content.

I also contributed to the visual consistency of the interface. When different parts of the UI, such as menus and the gameplay HUD, were individually well designed but followed different visual languages, I highlighted the inconsistency and helped establish a more unified stylistic direction.

When necessary, I used Photoshop and Illustrator to adjust existing assets or create small pieces of production-ready content.

3D Art and Environment

I assisted 3D artists with asset import, implementation and organization inside Unreal Engine. This included advice on naming conventions, transformations, pivots, materials and production practices that could affect performance or maintainability.

I also helped communicate engine-specific constraints and good practices, such as  using techniques like mesh instancing when appropriate for repeated environmental elements.

Animation

I worked with the animator to define which animations were needed from both a visual and logical perspective. This included distinguishing between one-shot animations, loops, idle animations and static poses, as well as determining when to use Blend Spaces, Aim Offsets and other animation tools.

I was also responsible for the character animation pipeline inside Unreal Engine, particularly the Animation Blueprint and the relationship between gameplay states, movement data and the final animated result.

Sound Design

I worked with the sound designer to explain gameplay states and determine the most appropriate structure for each audio behavior.

Depending on the feature, we evaluated whether the implementation required a single sound, a loop, multiple FMOD events or a shared event controlled through parameters. I also helped determine whether those parameters should be local to a specific object or global to the game state.

Technical Art

My involvement in technical art was more limited, but I contributed to optimization discussions and produced a small number of shaders and Niagara effects. I also supported communication between visual goals and technical constraints when a feature required cooperation between programmers and artists.

Across all these disciplines, my main contribution was to ensure that assets and ideas were not created in isolation, but were prepared and integrated according to the requirements of the complete game.

sections.title: WORLD STRUCTURE, OPTIMIZATION AND CONTENT PIPELINE
sections.html:
I was involved in the technical organization of the main game world and in maintaining a level structure that remained manageable as the amount of content increased.

I managed and reviewed the use of Unreal Engine’s World Partition system and HLOD configuration. This work required considering how the world was divided, loaded and represented at different distances, while also supporting the needs of environment production and gameplay.

I was also responsible for organizing the Outliner of the primary game level. A consistent hierarchy and naming structure were important because the level contained content produced by multiple disciplines, including gameplay actors, environment assets, lighting, audio, effects and progression logic.

The objective was to ensure that the game world remained understandable, editable and performant rather than becoming increasingly difficult to manage as new content was added.

sections.title: SOURCE CONTROL, BUILDS AND INTEGRATION
sections.html:
I was one of the main people responsible for source-control support and project integration within the team.

I helped team members use the project’s Git Flow workflow correctly, including updating branches, creating and pushing commits, merging changes and keeping local work aligned with the shared repository.

Since the project combined contributions from programmers, designers, artists and other disciplines, conflicts were not limited to source-code files. Unreal Engine assets, levels, configuration files and project settings could also produce integration problems.

I resolved the majority of merge conflicts and regularly combined the work produced across different branches into updated project versions.

I was also responsible for creating most of the internal builds. This process included:

  • collecting and merging the latest work from the team;
  • resolving conflicts and incompatible changes;
  • verifying that required assets were included;
  • checking that the project compiled and packaged correctly;
  • identifying issues caused by interactions between independently developed features;
  • providing the team with a stable version for testing.

This responsibility gave me visibility over the complete state of the project rather than only the systems I had personally implemented.

One of my priorities was to ensure that each new build represented an improvement over the previous one. Unexpected bugs could still occur, but deliberate changes were expected to preserve or improve the quality, usability and coherence already achieved.

My role was therefore not simply to produce code on an individual branch, but to ensure that the work of the entire team could regularly become a functioning, testable and distributable product.

sections.title: STEAMWORKS, DISTRIBUTION AND STORE PRESENCE
sections.html:
I managed most of the activities related to Steamworks and the game’s presence on Steam.

I created the Steamworks application and configured the settings required for the project. I also handled a large part of the technical preparation necessary to upload, test and distribute builds through the platform.

My responsibilities included:

  • Steamworks application setup;
  • configuration of the required store and application settings;
  • build and depot preparation;
  • Steam integration testing;
  • achievement and cloud-save related implementation;
  • verification of packaged builds;
  • preparation of materials for the Steam store page.

I also contributed directly to the public presentation of the game. I created or edited screenshots and animated GIFs, selected gameplay material and worked on the written descriptions used to explain the project to potential players.

This required balancing different priorities from those involved in internal development. Store content needed to communicate the identity of the game clearly, represent the actual gameplay accurately and present the project professionally while respecting Steam’s technical requirements.

Through this work, my contribution extended from the internal architecture of the game to its packaging, distribution and public-facing presentation.

sections.title: IMPACT ON THE FINAL PRODUCT
sections.html:
My work influenced Negative Edge across multiple layers of production.

Core Gameplay Foundation

I built a significant part of the technical foundation supporting movement, camera behavior, aiming, traversal, interaction, combat resources, encounters and game-state management.

Robust Gameplay Progression

I transformed design concepts and initial scripted sequences into gameplay flows capable of handling alternative player behavior, preventing progression issues and reducing the risk of soft locks and hard locks.

Greater Designer Autonomy

The systems and tools I developed allowed designers to prototype encounters, world events and progression logic directly inside Unreal Engine without requiring custom programming for every individual case.

Cross-Disciplinary Integration

I helped ensure that gameplay logic, animations, sound, UI, 2D assets and 3D content could work together inside a consistent technical structure.

Technical Risk Management

I evaluated new ideas according to their complexity, dependencies, performance implications and production cost, helping the team choose solutions that balanced ambition with the available time and resources.

Stable Builds and Continuous Integration

By managing merges, resolving conflicts and producing updated builds, I helped transform work developed across separate branches and disciplines into stable versions that could be tested by the complete team.

Product Consistency

I contributed to maintaining coherence between gameplay, visual presentation, usability and technical performance, encouraging each new iteration to improve rather than unintentionally weaken the existing product.

Release Readiness

I supported the game through its complete production pipeline, from early technical architecture and feature development to Steamworks configuration, packaging and store-page presentation.

Overall, my role was to connect the creative vision of the team with the technical and production work required to make it real. I was responsible not only for building systems, but also for ensuring that the contributions of every discipline could converge into a coherent, stable and achievable final product.

title: State Machine System
description: A plugin for Unreal Engine, available on the FAB Marketplace, originally developed during the production of The Negative Edge. It is a generic state machine system for Actors that supports concurrent state machines, custom transitions, input-driven logic, and seamless Blueprint/C++ integration.
aboutHtml:
A plugin for Unreal Engine, available on the FAB Marketplace, originally developed during the production of The Negative Edge.

It is a generic state machine system for Actors that supports concurrent state machines, custom transitions, input-driven logic, and seamless Blueprint/C++ integration.

Includes a sample project with a playable character demonstrating movement, jumping, shooting, and state communication.

sections.title: System UML Overview
sections.html: This UML diagram provides an overview of the system. The idea is to have an ActorComponent called StateMachineComponent to manage individual states, as well as a MultiStateMachineComponent that can run multiple StateMachineComponents simultaneously, enabling more complex state management.

title: GVUEDK-Framework
description: GVUEDK (Gabevlogd Unreal Engine Development Kit) is my personal framework for Unreal Engine development. It provides a collection of modular systems and utilities that I’ve built to streamline game development, prototyping, and 3C programming.
aboutHtml:
GVUEDK (Gabevlogd Unreal Engine Development Kit) is my personal framework for Unreal Engine development.
It provides a collection of modular systems and utilities that I’ve built to streamline game development, prototyping, and 3C programming.

title: Weabot
description: An environmental puzzle game where the character is a small flying drone with the ability to take control of other robots encountered along its path, using them to overcome environmental puzzles that block its way.
aboutHtml:
It is an environmental puzzle game. The main character is a small flying drone with the ability to take control of other robots encountered along its path, using them to overcome environmental puzzles that block its way.

We created a demo consisting of 4 levels (the first one is a tutorial) with a duration of about 40-60 minutes (depending on how much the player explores, as there are hidden optional puzzles in the levels).

You can find the last version of the game at the bottom of this page.

sections.title: What Is Gamelab?
sections.html:
GameLabs are collaborative workshops where students from all disciplines—Game Design, Game Programming, 3D Art, and 2D Art—form teams to create a game from scratch (create a remake of an existing game for first-year students). These teams are given a set development time to complete and deliver a final product, including a pitch for a hypothetical publisher, simulating a real-world work environment.

Students are expected to self-organize and collaborate effectively to bring the game to completion. The only "rule" is that they must use development management tools (such as Trello or Jira) to track and organize the entire creative process, and apply Git version control with proper GitFlow practices.

sections.title: Main Personal Contribute
sections.html:
In our team, there were two programmers, and we divided the various systems equally. The main systems I worked on include:

  • Multiple controllable character system: the system that manages the core game mechanic, allowing players to control multiple characters through an inheritance-based structure.
  • Health System: A generic character health management system and the relative UI.
  • Throwing System: Component gives the player the ability to aim and throw equipped items.
  • Chaos Implementation: Setted up unreal engine’s chaos system for every destructible object of the game.
  • Highlighting System: A system for highlighting all interactables objects of the game when the player is close to them.
  • Chest System: A chest system with fixed drops, for collecting in-game items. And the relative UI when collecting new items.
  • Items: All the items that give the player offensive and defensive abilities.

sections.title: UE5 and Blueprints
sections.html:
This was my first project in Unreal Engine, and consequently, my first time working with Blueprints. After six months of using them, I’ve come to the conclusion that they are an extremely powerful tool, especially during the initial prototyping phase. However, to achieve a clean, organized, and—most importantly—easily maintainable result over time, it’s essential to complement them with good old C++. As projects grow in complexity, there's a serious risk of getting lost in a tangled mess of nodes that becomes increasingly difficult to manage.

The class shown below is the base class from which all controllable characters (including the little main drone) inherit.

  1. Unreal Events
    Begin Play: Just a basic initialization of the pawn, caching some external references.
  2. Movement
    Input actions from Unreal Enhanced Input System for translation and rotation input.
  3. Interactable Detection
    Begin/end ovelap component events for the detection of all interactables (interface) nears the player. Basically a simple sphere collider.
  4. Damage
    Default Unreal's event for damage applying
  5. Custom Events
    • TryPossessNewEntity: event for trying to control a new robot. empty because each character needs specific implementation.
    • PossessNewEntity: Event for the actual transition to controlling a new robot, in Unreal terms, assigning a new pawn to the player controller.
    • PossessionStarted: Called on every robots when the player start to controlling them.
    • PossessionEnded: Called on every robots when the player stop to controlling them.
    • Respawn: Event used for respawning a robot.
  6. Health Manager Events
    Callbacks from the custom actor component Health Manager, primarily used to trigger VFX associated with various events (damage, regeneration, etc.). The actual health management is handled within the component's Blueprint.

title: Push Sushi
description: Remake of the original IPhone game Push Sushi. Is a mobile puzzle game where the player must solve puzzles by sliding sushi blocks on a grid to clear a path and free the main piece of the grid.
aboutHtml:
Remake of the original IPhone game Push Sushi.
Is a mobile puzzle game where the player must solve puzzles by sliding sushi blocks on a grid to clear a path and free the main piece of the grid.

The game features appealing graphics, an intuitive user interface, and a progressively challenging difficulty curve that keeps the player engaged and motivated to solve increasingly complex levels.

sections.title: What Is Gamelab?
sections.html:
GameLabs are collaborative workshops where students from all disciplines—Game Design, Game Programming, 3D Art, and 2D Art—form teams to create a game from scratch (create a remake of an existing game for first-year students). These teams are given a set development time to complete and deliver a final product, including a pitch for a hypothetical publisher, simulating a real-world work environment.

Students are expected to self-organize and collaborate effectively to bring the game to completion. The only "rule" is that they must use development management tools (such as Trello or Jira) to track and organize the entire creative process, and apply Git version control with proper GitFlow practices.

sections.title: Main Personal Contribute
sections.html:
As the only programmer of the project, I developed all the game features. Below a list of the main systems and features developed:

  • Pawns Movement System: The game pieces can be dragged by sliding the finger along the direction in which they are oriented while remaining within the limits of the map or within those determined by the other pieces on the field.
  • Undo Moves System: In the HUD there is a button that cancels the last move. It is possible to go back in the moves up to the initial configuration of the playing field.
  • Score System: System that calculates the score at the end of each game based on the number of moves made and on the hints and undos used to solve the level.
  • Switch Theme System: Each theme has a different HUD. The switch theme system will have to manage the change of the hud theme based on the theme choice made by the player in the menu.
  • UI System: All the game menus and the HUD.
  • Level Editor Tool: Tool for fast creation of game levels.

sections.title: Tool Programming
sections.html:
In this project, I put into practice for the first time what I had just learned about creating tools in Unity. The goal was to build a level editor tool to speed up the game's level creation process. The outcome was very successful, as the designer was able to create 30 levels in about a week.

The tool functionality are:

  • New Level: automatically opens a new scene with an empty grid ready to be filled
  • Save Level: automatically creates a Scriptable Object, with the current grid configuration, Used for the runtime loading of different levels within the same Unity scene.
  • Pawns Positioning: to fill the grid just select the wanted pawns from the mesh list, select the coordinates on the toogles' grid, click "place pawn" to position the selected pawn and "back" to undo the last positioning.

Level editor tool usage example

sections.html: Play the WebGL version directly in the browser. On Android, the APK is available from the download link above.

title: HLSL Shader
description: Assignment for the end of the shading module: HLSL on Unity.
aboutHtml:
Here's my assignment for the end of the shading module in HLSL on Unity. Assignment characteristics:

Create a crystal shader that must have:

  • transparency
  • refraction
  • rim light

sections.title: Code
sections.html:
Shader "Custom/Crystal"
{
    Properties
    {
        _FresnelColor("Fresnel Color", Color) = (1, 1, 1, 1)
        _Color("Color", Color) = (1, 1, 1, 1)
        _FresnelIntensity("Fresnel Intensity", Range(0, 10)) = 1
        _FresnelPower("Fresnel Power", Range(0, 10)) = 1
        _RefractionIndex("Refraction Index", Range(-1, 1)) = 1
        _Intensity("Refraction Intensity", Range(0, 1)) = 1
    }



    SubShader
    {
        // Grab the current screen content into _GrabTexture
        GrabPass{ "_GrabTexture" }

        Tags {"Queue" = "Transparent"}
        LOD 100

        Pass
        {
            CGPROGRAM
            #pragma vertex vert
            #pragma fragment frag

            #include "UnityCG.cginc"

            struct appdata
            {
                float4 vertex : POSITION;
                float3 normal : NORMAL;
            };

            struct v2f
            {
                float4 vertex : SV_POSITION;
                float4 grabPos : TEXTCORD0;
                float3 normal : TEXCOORD1;
                float3 viewDir : TEXCOORD2;
            };

            float2 ProjectDirectionToClipSpace(float3 direction)
            {
                // Convert the direction vector to homogeneous coordinates (w = 0 for direction vectors)
                float4 directionHomogeneous = float4(direction, 0.0);

                // Transform the direction from local space to view space
                float4 viewSpaceDirection = mul(UNITY_MATRIX_V, directionHomogeneous);

                // Transform the direction from view space to clip space
                float4 clipSpaceDirection = mul(UNITY_MATRIX_P, viewSpaceDirection);

                // Convert to 2D by taking the x and y components
                float2 clipSpace2D = clipSpaceDirection.xy;

                return clipSpace2D;
            }

            sampler2D _GrabTexture;

            float4 _FresnelColor;
            float4 _Color;

            float _FresnelIntensity;
            float _FresnelPower;
            float _RefractionIndex;
            float _Intensity;

            v2f vert(appdata v)
            {
                v2f o;
                o.vertex = UnityObjectToClipPos(v.vertex);
                o.grabPos = ComputeGrabScreenPos(o.vertex);
                o.normal = UnityObjectToWorldNormal(v.normal);
                o.viewDir = WorldSpaceViewDir(v.vertex);
                return o;
            }

            fixed4 frag(v2f i) : SV_Target
            {
                //normalize in the frag for a smoothest result
                i.viewDir = normalize(i.viewDir);
                i.normal = normalize(i.normal);

                //-------------- refraction ----------------
                float3 refractDir = refract(-i.viewDir, i.normal, _RefractionIndex);
                float2 reftactDirClipSpace = ProjectDirectionToClipSpace(refractDir);
                float2 grabUV = (i.grabPos.xy / i.grabPos.w) + (reftactDirClipSpace * _Intensity);
                //avoid the stretching of the grab texture at the edges by repeating it instead
                grabUV = grabUV - floor(grabUV);
                float4 grabColor = tex2D(_GrabTexture, grabUV);
                //------------ refraction end --------------

                //---------------- fresnel -----------------
                float NdotV = dot(i.normal, i.viewDir);
                float fresnelAmount = 1 - max(0, NdotV);
                float4 fresnelColor = pow(fresnelAmount, _FresnelPower) * _FresnelIntensity * _FresnelColor;
                //-------------- fresnel end ---------------

                float4 outColor = lerp(fresnelColor, grabColor * _Color, NdotV);
                return outColor;
            }

            ENDCG
        }
    }
}

title: Sandwich!
description: Remake of the original mobile game Sandwich!. Is a casual mobile puzzle game where players stack ingredients to create sandwiches
aboutHtml:
Here's my assignment for the end of the Mobile Development module.
Assignment characteristics:
Recreate the original mobile game Sandwich!.

Sandwich! is a casual mobile puzzle game where players stack ingredients to create sandwiches.
The goal is to fold different layers of ingredients in the correct sequence, stacking everything from cheese to vegetables and meats, until a complete sandwich is made.
Simple swipe controls make it easy to fold the ingredients over each other, but careful planning is required to avoid mistakes.

sections.title: Game Loop
sections.html:
1. The GameManager initially loads the PauseState, within which it waits for the player's input to start the game. Additionally, at the game's start (in the Start of LevelLoader), the first level is already instantiated.
  2. Once the player provides the input to start the game, the state transitions to PlayState.
  3. Entering PlayState enables the SwipesManager, which handles reading player input to move various elements on the game grid.
  4. For each valid move made, the win condition is checked; if satisfied, the state changes to WinState.
  5. Entering WinState disables the SwipesManager and waits for the player to input a command to proceed to the next level.
  6. Once the player inputs the command to advance to the next level, it returns to PlayState.
  7. Upon exiting WinState, the LoadNextLevel event is triggered. In this event, the LevelLoader loads the new level (using LevelGenerator to generate random levels whenever it's triggered), and all elements of the previous level's grid self-destruct.
Communication between the various classes is primarily managed through static event Actions.

sections.title: Stack Animation
sections.html:
Definition: from = stack to be rotated (Target Stack);
Definition: to = stack that receives from.
Definition: stackCount = height of the stack.

There are three possible animation cases for a Stack:

  • stackCount_from == stackCount_to;
  • stackCount_from < stackCount_to;
  • stackCount_from > stackCount_to.
Graphic illustration of the three cases mentioned above:

The StacksAnimator is the class responsible for calculating the Starting Point, Ending Point, and Rotation Pivot, and performing the movement of the Target Stack using a classic state pattern after calculating the necessary data.

title: Tool Programming
description: Assignment for the end of the module: Tool Programming on Unity.
aboutHtml:
Here's my assignment for the end of the Tool Programming on Unity module. Assignment characteristics:

Create a level editor tool with the following features:

  • Interface from which to select the prefab
  • Preview of the prefab in the game viewport, following the mouse cursor
  • Ability to rotate the preview of the selected prefab by 90 degrees at a time
  • Spawning and snapping of the prefab at the entrance of the wall it is aligned with
  • Undo system

sections.title: Preview's Positioning in 3D Space
sections.html:
The prefab preview needs to follow the mouse, so to calculate this position, it's fairly straightforward to understand that a raycast is required.
This raycast originates from the camera's position and is directed toward the mouse pointer (a function already provided by Unity).
Now that we have the direction, the key is determining at what distance from the camera the intersection point with the terrain lies along this path.

The first idea, which I initially implemented, was to spawn an invisible plane and simply get the exact point by detecting where the raycast hits.
This works, but it's not a very elegant solution.

If we think about the problem for a moment, it's not too difficult to realize that the camera's position and the point we want to calculate are the two vertices of the acute angles in a right triangle. Therefore, the distance we need to calculate is simply the hypotenuse of this triangle.
Once this is understood, the solution becomes straightforward, as trigonometry tells us that:

hypotenuse = Cam.position.y / cos(α), where cos(α) = Cam.downward • Raycast.direction. 

private void UpdatePreviewPosition()
{
    Ray ray = HandleUtility.GUIPointToWorldRay(Event.current.mousePosition);
    if (Physics.Raycast(ray, out RaycastHit hit) && hit.collider.CompareTag("SnapPoint") && _enablePreviewSnapping)
    {
        TrySnapPreviewPosition(hit, ray);
    }
    else
    {
        _previewPosition = CalculatePreviewPosition(ray);
    }           
}               
/// Calculate where to locate the preview in the 3D world space based on the camera and mouse pointer position
/// param name="ray" ray that starts from the camera position and go through the mouse pointer
private Vector3 CalculatePreviewPosition(Ray ray)
{
    // H = Y / D  where H = hypotenuse , Y = cathetus ,  D = Cos(angle between Y and H)
    float hypotenuse = ray.origin.y / Vector3.Dot(Vector3.down, ray.direction);
    return ray.origin + ray.direction * hypotenuse + Vector3.up * _spawnPositionHeight;
}

sections.title: Preview Snapping
sections.html:
The reasoning I followed for this feature is as follows:

Each prefab is a SnappableObject, which has a list of snap points (i.e., a list of Transforms), each accompanied by a collider. We define the direction from the center of a SnappableObject to one of its snap points as Dn.

This means that if a SnappableObject has four snap points, we can calculate D1, D2, D3, and D4.

Based on this definition, we can say that two snap points from two different SnappableObjects (Dx and Dy) are aligned if the dot product between them equals 1. 

private void TrySnapPreviewPosition(RaycastHit snapPoint, Ray ray)
{
    _selectedObject.TryGetComponent(out SnappableObject snappableObject);
                
    //this vector is a direction that goes from the center of the snappableObject
    //present in the scene to its snap point (door) currently inspected by the mouse pointer
    Vector3 direction = snapPoint.transform.parent.rotation * snapPoint.transform.localPosition.normalized;
                
    //(if doors are aligned)
    if (CanSnap(direction, snappableObject))
    {
        _previewPosition = snapPoint.transform.position;
    }
    else
    {
        _previewPosition = CalculatePreviewPosition(ray);
    }
}               
private bool CanSnap(Vector3 snapDirection, SnappableObject snappableObj)
{
    foreach(Transform trs in snappableObj.SnapPoint)
    {
        if (Vector3.Dot(snapDirection, _previewRotation * trs.localPosition.normalized) < -0.9f)
            return true;
    }
    return false;
}
